title: From Hollywood Makeup Artist to Clean Beauty Pioneer: Laura Linsenmeyer's Story
guestBio: Laura Linsenmeyer is a clean beauty pioneer and founder of ROOTS the Beauty Underground, based in Laguna Beach. A former film and TV makeup artist, she transitioned from Hollywood to entrepreneurship to create a purpose-driven, non-toxic beauty brand that champions community and conscious living.
excerpt: Discover how Laura Linsenmeyer left her Hollywood makeup career to build ROOTS the Beauty Underground, a clean beauty brand that's redefining what it means to lead with purpose. This episode reveals the courage it takes to build a conscious business on your own terms.
metaDescription: Clean beauty entrepreneur Laura Linsenmeyer shares how she built ROOTS Beauty from Hollywood roots, balancing motherhood and mission-driven leadership.
keyTakeaways: Transitioning from a creative career to entrepreneurship requires courage to follow your values over security.
Clean beauty isn't just about products—it's about creating a movement toward conscious living.
Building a purpose-driven business means prioritizing community and values alongside profit.
Motherhood can amplify your mission and drive you to create positive change in the world.
Leading a woman-powered brand requires intentional cultivation of supportive relationships.
Authenticity in business comes from aligning your personal values with your professional mission.
Success in conscious entrepreneurship is measured by impact, not just revenue.

pullQuotes.text: Clean beauty isn't just about what we put on our skin—it's about the values we choose to live by
When you lead from the inside out, your business becomes an extension of who you are
Building a conscious brand means choosing courage over comfort every single day

content:
What does it take to leave the glittering world of Hollywood makeup artistry and dive headfirst into the conscious beauty movement? According to Laura Linsenmeyer, founder of ROOTS the Beauty Underground, it takes a combination of courage, conviction, and an unwavering commitment to your values. In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, host Aimee Rickabus sits down with Laura to explore the intersection of entrepreneurship, motherhood, and mission-driven leadership.

Laura's story begins in the fast-paced world of film and television, where she worked as a makeup artist creating looks for celebrities and productions. While the work was creative and exciting, she began to question the products she was using daily—not just their impact on her clients' skin, but their broader environmental and health implications. This awakening wasn't sudden; it was a gradual shift that eventually became impossible to ignore.

The Leap from Security to Purpose

Making the transition from a steady career in entertainment to launching her own beauty brand required Laura to confront her deepest fears about security and success. You know that feeling when you're comfortable but not fulfilled—Laura lived in that space for years before deciding that alignment with her values mattered more than a predictable paycheck. Her decision to launch ROOTS wasn't just about creating another beauty brand; it was about pioneering a movement toward conscious consumption and non-toxic living.

The early days of building ROOTS required Laura to become comfortable with uncertainty while staying true to her vision. She discovered that entrepreneurship isn't just about having a great product idea—it's about having the resilience to weather the inevitable storms that come with building something from scratch. Every decision, from sourcing ingredients to packaging design, became an opportunity to reinforce her commitment to clean beauty principles.

Redefining Clean Beauty

Throughout the conversation, Laura challenges the conventional understanding of what clean beauty actually means. It's not just about avoiding harmful chemicals or using organic ingredients—though those elements are certainly important. For Laura, clean beauty represents a holistic approach to wellness that encompasses environmental responsibility, ethical sourcing, and community building. It's about creating products that honor both the people who use them and the planet that provides the raw materials.

This philosophy extends to every aspect of how ROOTS operates as a business. Laura emphasizes that being a conscious brand means making decisions that might not always be the most profitable in the short term but align with long-term values and sustainability. She's learned that customers who care about clean beauty are also customers who care about how businesses treat their employees, their communities, and their environmental impact.

The conversation reveals how Laura has navigated the challenges of educating consumers about clean beauty while building a profitable business. She's discovered that authenticity resonates more than perfection—customers want to see the real journey, including the mistakes and learning experiences that come with building a purpose-driven company.

Community as the Foundation

One of the most striking aspects of Laura's approach to business is her emphasis on community building. Rather than viewing ROOTS simply as a product company, she's created a platform for like-minded women to connect around shared values of wellness and conscious living. This community-first approach has become a cornerstone of her business strategy and a source of personal fulfillment.

Laura shares how being based in Laguna Beach has influenced her understanding of community and tradition. The area's emphasis on environmental consciousness and holistic wellness has provided both inspiration and a testing ground for her products and values. She's learned that building a local community first creates a strong foundation for broader growth and impact.

The discussion touches on how Laura has cultivated relationships with other female entrepreneurs and business owners, creating a network of support that extends far beyond traditional business partnerships. She's discovered that leading a woman-powered brand means intentionally creating space for other women to thrive, whether they're employees, customers, or fellow business owners.

Motherhood as a Leadership Amplifier

Perhaps the most personal part of the conversation focuses on how motherhood has shaped Laura's approach to leadership and business building. Rather than seeing her role as a mother as something that competes with her entrepreneurial ambitions, she's found that motherhood has amplified her sense of purpose and urgency around creating positive change.

Laura explains how becoming a mother made her even more committed to creating non-toxic products and promoting conscious living. The desire to leave a better world for her children has become a driving force behind every business decision. This perspective has also influenced how she thinks about the legacy she wants to build with ROOTS—not just as a successful company, but as a catalyst for broader change in the beauty industry.

The conversation explores the practical realities of balancing motherhood with running a growing business. Laura is refreshingly honest about the challenges while also highlighting the unique strengths that mothers bring to entrepreneurship—the ability to multitask, prioritize effectively, and maintain perspective on what truly matters.

Leading from the Inside Out

Throughout the episode, a central theme emerges around what it means to lead from the inside out. For Laura, this means ensuring that her personal values and business practices are in complete alignment, even when that alignment requires difficult choices or sacrifices. It's about creating a business that feels like an authentic expression of who she is rather than a separate entity she manages.

This approach to leadership extends to how Laura treats her team and customers. She's committed to creating a work environment that reflects her values around wellness, community, and purpose. The result is a company culture that attracts people who are genuinely passionate about the mission, creating a positive feedback loop that strengthens both the business and its impact.

Laura's story offers a roadmap for anyone considering the leap from traditional employment to purpose-driven entrepreneurship. Her journey demonstrates that building a conscious business requires courage, persistence, and an unwavering commitment to your values—but the rewards extend far beyond financial success to include personal fulfillment and positive impact on the world around you.

quiz.title: What's Your Change-Making Style?
quiz.description: Inspired by Laura Linsenmeyer's journey from Hollywood makeup artist to clean beauty pioneer, discover how you approach creating meaningful change in your life and career.

questions.question: When you feel misaligned with your current situation, what's your typical response?

options.text: I start planning my exit strategy pretty quickly – life's too short to stay somewhere that doesn't feel right
I look for small ways to make improvements while keeping my foundation stable
I try to find others who share my concerns and see if we can create change together
I reflect deeply on what's missing and how I can bring more of my true self to the situation

questions.question: What motivates you most when making a big career or life decision?

options.text: The excitement of possibility and the chance to create something new
Finding the sweet spot between growth and security
The opportunity to make a positive impact on my community or the world
Ensuring the decision reflects who I really am and what I value most

questions.question: When facing uncertainty in your professional life, you tend to:

options.text: Embrace it as part of the adventure – uncertainty means possibility
Create structure and plans to help navigate through the unknown
Lean on my network and community for support and guidance
Use it as an opportunity for self-reflection and clarifying my values

questions.question: How do you typically approach building something new in your life or career?

options.text: I dive in headfirst and figure it out as I go – momentum creates clarity
I research thoroughly and build step by step with careful milestones
I focus on how it can serve others and build community around the vision
I make sure every element reflects my authentic self and core beliefs

questions.question: What does success look like to you?

options.text: Having the freedom to pursue my passions and create on my own terms
Achieving sustainable growth while maintaining stability and security
Making a measurable positive difference in the lives of others
Living in complete alignment with my values across all areas of life

questions.question: When you think about leadership, you believe it's most important to:

options.text: Be willing to go first and show others what's possible through bold action
Create reliable systems and steady progress that others can depend on
Unite people around a shared vision and empower them to contribute
Model authenticity and encourage others to bring their whole selves forward

results.title: The Courageous Leaper
results.description: You're not afraid to leave behind what's comfortable when it no longer aligns with your values. Like Laura leaving her Hollywood career, you trust your instincts and are willing to take big risks for big rewards. You lead with your heart and create change through bold action. Laura's story will inspire you to trust your leap even more and show you how to build something meaningful from scratch.

results.title: The Steady Builder
results.description: You prefer to create change gradually, building momentum through consistent action and careful planning. You value security but also want purpose, and you're skilled at finding ways to bridge both. Your methodical approach to transformation is a strength that leads to lasting impact. This episode will show you how to honor your need for stability while still pursuing meaningful change.

results.title: The Mission Amplifier
results.description: You're energized by causes bigger than yourself and naturally rally others around shared values. Like Laura building a community around clean beauty, you understand that real change happens through connection and collective action. You measure success by the positive impact you create in the world. Laura's approach to building a purpose-driven business will resonate deeply with your values-first mindset.

results.title: The Authentic Integrator
results.description: You excel at weaving together different aspects of your life – career, values, relationships, and personal growth – into one cohesive whole. You don't believe in compartmentalizing and seek alignment in everything you do. Your superpower is living authentically across all areas of life. Laura's story about aligning her personal values with her professional mission will inspire you to integrate even more boldly.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Let's have some fun. Today I am so excited to welcome someone I've known since our middle school days. The hilarious, whip smart, and fiercely intentional Laura Linsenmeyer. Laura is the founder of Roots: The Beauty Underground, a clean beauty haven in Laguna Beach that's helped people detox their skincare routines and reconnect with what's real, effective, and healthy, all without losing the glamour. She's a leading voice in clean beauty space, and her work is rooted, no pun intended, in integrity, science, and stunning aesthetics.

Laura is also a resilient small business owner who navigated the storm of COVID with creativity and grit, all the while staying true to her mission. She's had a peek at chapter 1 of my new book, and I can't wait to dive into her story, From Startup to Standout, with all of you. Get ready for some laughs, some truth bombs, and a whole lot of inspiration. All right, Laura. So good to have you here today.

**Laura:** Absolutely. Thanks for having me.

**Aimee:** Let's start with your journey and the creation of Roots. Like a lot of our listeners, I'm hoping are getting inspired and they're getting ideas and they're thinking about, okay, this is cool. I have this idea, but how do I start? So my first question for you is before Roots became an iconic clean beauty destination and it was just an idea, could you walk us through how it all began?

**Laura:** So interesting. I really tap into the spirituality of all of it. I really feel divinely guided, each step along the way. I feel like I always knew I've always been in the beauty industry. Went to beauty school right out of high school. So just always been in the beauty industry and never quite knew what I was going to do, kind of finding my way.

I learned about clean beauty early on in like early 2000s in LA. I learned about organic hair color and I was doing hair in LA, met these girls. They were opening a salon using organic hair color and I was like, "Wait, what's that? Wait a minute. Tell me more." And then I started down the rabbit hole of going, "Wait, why is this clean? What do you mean? What's the opposite of that?"

And just starting to do my research and I couldn't believe there was no clean beauty hub. Like it was a gap in the market in this huge beauty industry with so much oversaturation. There's a gap in the market of something that's good for humanity, making a difference in the world. I just couldn't even believe it. So I was like, bingo, I know what I need to do now. And I really learned through that. It's not like I learned just with everybody else as I was building the business. I just knew it was the right move to do it.

And as far as the name Roots: The Beauty Underground, I mean, I thought of that when I was like 22 years old. It came to me and I was like, late nights I'd be like, I'm going to open this and it's going to be like this and I've been talking about this for 20 years. And then it was so fun when it actually happened and people would go, I remember when you were saying this 15 years ago. So lots of manifestation, speaking it into existence, which is crazy.

I feel like for us entrepreneurs, I see it as speaking things into existence. Other people can see it as like, oh, they say all these things and are they really ever going to do them? But it's very much we're both Sagittariuses. It's very much speak our reality into existence. And I feel like that's totally what's happened.

**Aimee:** Yeah. No, you're so right. Well, if you don't speak it first, how is it ever going to come into fruition? That's when the energy starts to pick up momentum. I mean, you have to talk about it because a lot of times you need other people to get on the same program to get around you. And the only way other people can get behind you is if they've heard about it, if you're communicating it. It's like learning to find your voice.

**Laura:** Totally. Yeah. So first find your voice. Exactly. And lock in on that intention. I was locked in on it. It wasn't like, well, should I and maybe and well, maybe I should do that. No, I locked in on it. Thank God. I don't know how. I just knew it was something that I needed to do.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. And that was so many years ago. Was that 13, 14 years ago?

**Laura:** The business has been operating for almost 13. And then the conception was like a year. So yeah, we're almost 14 years ago.

**Aimee:** Wow. That's so amazing. And it's come so far. And it's lasted through the biggest challenge of our lifetime. How did you cope? How did you pivot and adjust and conquer during COVID?

**Laura:** God, triggering, right? For real. Super triggering. It was so fascinating when that all hit and it was like shut down. The coolest thing, all of our clients jumped in and were like, "We want to make sure you don't go anywhere." The client support was insane. They came to us like I didn't need to be advertising and they straight up came up and said, "What do we need to do to keep buying from you and make sure you don't go anywhere?"

**Aimee:** Wow. So how did you set that up? Did you go online? Did you deliver to people's homes?

**Laura:** Yes, both. All of the above. I mean, what a wacky time. My whole system was off course. I would wake up at noon and stay at the shop till 2:00 in the morning. Just to-do lists and surviving. And I would leave the store at midnight and go drive around and hand deliver on doorsteps. And then take over to the post office and then we have a little Dutch door so we would have pickups in the door.

And that Thank God because deep conversations were that was nice. You do have your little Dutch door so people could come and just swing by, call you, say what they need and they can just swing by and pick it up.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Like a drive-thru.

**Laura:** Like a drive-thru. Yeah. That was intense. Way to pivot, right? And it takes such creativity and strength to make those changes and you never know when they're going to come in life, but it's all about how we can be resilient in business and to stay alive.

**Aimee:** Always. Literally.

**Laura:** Never once did I go, I quit. This is too hard. I could have definitely. I mean, I feel like I'm still recovering from all that. I burnt out my adrenals and all that time type of stuff. Never once did I feel like I couldn't pull it off. It was like I almost thrive in survival mode, unfortunately.

**Aimee:** Me, too. Me, too. I think that's why I have so many kids, right? Seriously, add another one to the plate and then write a book. Why not?

**Laura:** Totally. I feel like every time I have a baby, I start another company. I don't know. Laura, make me stop.

**Aimee:** That's cosmic. I know, right? That's very energetic. That's a thing. That's an actual thing for me. We've been looking at our companies and we're like, "Oh, we had a baby that year. We started a company that year. We had a baby that year. We started a company that year." And you can look at our rhythm and it's like there's something about that creative birthing process for us.

**Laura:** Yeah. And you're in the mode of... It's a mode that you're in.

**Aimee:** Wow. That's fascinating. Well, take me back to the inception. So like the actual ins and outs of, okay, I have this great idea. It's going to be a non-toxic beauty shop. I know what I'm going to call it. What do you do? Did you write a business plan? How did you find your investors?

**Laura:** Good question. Once I from that point, I went, "Okay, where am I not skilled? I needed corporate training." And so I was like, I'm going to get a job in a management position. I got a job at Bare Essentials way back when up in LA and I was like, okay, I'm just this is research. I'm doing research for my grander vision and being in that that was the cleanest company at the time. So I thought, okay, perfect. That's where I'll go.

And then really volunteering and being like, I want to go to every meeting. I want to go to every class. I want to learn everything, the ins, the outs, the operations, procedures. Thank God I did that. I wouldn't have pulled this off if I didn't do that at all.

**Aimee:** Isn't that amazing? See, that's great advice. If you don't know something, go to a big company and learn it. Learn. Go and just be like, and there's no rush. Why rush into something and then fumble it? I kind of did step by step and went, "Okay, all right. I feel good about this. I feel like I got it. Now I'll go to the next." I wouldn't have been able to pull that off without learning operations procedures, checklists, templates, understanding the creating the flow and creating... Yeah, I literally took every idea they had and just put my twist on it, right?

**Laura:** And then when opening roots straight up had a binder and had all the paper the documents and then made my own versions of everything.

**Aimee:** Amazing. Yeah because you do need all those operating procedures and manuals and employee manuals. How anybody would do it without it because even with all that preparation I had it still wasn't now it needs to be implemented and so even that implementation process was chaotic and hectic and there were so many balls in the air and I was kind of more prepared so I was like whoa anybody who's jumping into especially retail without that organization I do not recommend that.

**Laura:** Yes. Yes. Retail needs real You really need to be organized. You need to have your ducks in a row. Almost need a master class on this. You can put one together. I would love to do that. I've actually from all the stuff I've created of the organization. I've did it with the frame of mind of if I were to sell this or if I were to I could if somebody came to me, which some people have, but things didn't pan out. They were like, "I want to open a roots. I want to open a roots in my town." And I was like, "Well, I got the system. It's all down to events, down to how to process returns, how to deal with it. The psychology of sales, training your staff. I have all this documented and I know I'm going to do something with it one day."

**Aimee:** Neat. I love that. Well, if you ever need help here. Perfect. Noted. I definitely would love to come in and help you with that. That sounds like a blast because it is a gift. It's not every We live in such a blessed place. Not every town has their non-toxic beauty shop they can go into and have somebody do your makeup and make you look pretty and do it in a non-toxic way and send you home with all these goodies that are going to be safe for you to use on yourself and even on your children, your teenagers. Even my four-year-old has her makeup from your shop.

**Laura:** I know. I was like, we're not gonna get the stuff from CVS.

**Aimee:** No. Gross. And what's in those things? Could you give me your top I want to hear the dirty dozen. So sad. Especially thinking about the kids. There are children that are putting the heavy metals with their little ovaries and their little organs forming. God, it's criminal. It really is. And it makes me mad. Read me the dirty dozen, please.

**Laura:** So I mean, this is the top 12. There's over 1,400 chemicals, but this is just America's top 12. BHA, BHT, coal tar dyes, DEA, formaldehyde releasing preservatives. I would say the number one is fragrance. 100% synthetic fragrance got to go. It's linked to anything and everything. Throwing off your hormones, hair loss, infertility. I mean, everything. It's synthetic fragrance must be banned, period.

**Aimee:** Wow. Well, I noticed a huge difference in my health. My 15-year-old was like, "Let's go get fragrance, Mom." And I was like, "Okay." So we went and got fragrance, and I haven't worn real life perfume in 15 years. I started wearing it a month ago. I've been sick three times and I haven't been sick in 15 years. And so I looked at it after I watched a video. I saw a reel on how toxic it is and I was like never again. What was I thinking? I just wanted to smell pretty. It's insane. I didn't realize I was absolutely affecting my immune system.

**Laura:** Well, and that's the thing, too. It's such an array of toxic chemicals that it's going to affect everybody differently. And I feel like that's why it can be slipped through the cracks because everybody's going to get a different depending on maybe where what you're weak in what your unique body's weaknesses are. It's going to attack those which is going to show up differently in you than it's going to show up in you. So you're not seeing one side effect. It is unreal.

And there will be clients that walk in, new clients, this is always a new client, and they walk in, there's a particular whether it's a vibration, it's something in their microbiome of their skin. I can see and I'm like, you need to remove all toxic or all synthetic fragrance from your house. I can see it in their skin, in the follicles of their hair. It's a very particular look.

**Aimee:** Wow. That's next level. So after fragrance, what's next as they stopped you?

**Laura:** Sodium lauryl sulfate which a lot of the greenwashing companies have caught on to and so oh sodium lauryl sulfate free buy us and it's like but you didn't clean out the other 75 toxic chemicals in your stuff but you took this one thing out there's so much trickery there's so much deceit but yeah sodium lauryl sulfate's one that a lot of the it's caught on a lot of people know not to get shampoo with that. So now they took it out which speaks volumes for the people have the power. We say we're not going to buy something they change it. That's the root. I'm always like how can we change the world and it's hit them in their pockets. And knowledge is power.

**Aimee:** That's right. So if we educate then we can advocate for change.

**Laura:** Yeah. Totally. And I really feel like that's what the big movement is right now. The big push to get the food dyes out. It's like, yeah, okay, we're done with the toxic rainbow. Bye-bye. See you later. I can't believe we had those petroleum based food flavorings in our children's food for 60 years, 50 years. It's unbelievable. Are we supposed to still listen to the organization and the people that allowed that to go through? What's happening here? And who's being held accountable? And how is this going to make sure this never happens again? How do we make sure these chemicals aren't just renamed?

That's the thing. These I feel like these psychopaths who have all these chemicals that they need to put somewhere they can't dump them anymore byproducts of the petroleum industry. So basically create a byproduct and then create a market for the byproduct and then it's instead of having to pay someone to take away your waste you can pay someone to buy it to use it as something else and we have to stop it demonic.

**Aimee:** Well it's really really sad. We should not be eating petroleum based anything, food colors, food flavorings, or anything. There should be no petroleum in our food. I'm sorry. It's food. It should be organic. And I think that that's just a really basic ask as a human to say that I have a basic right to eat organic food. Food that does meaning organic just means food without petroleum in it. That's it. That's what it means. That's what organic means. And that's a very basic thing I don't think people understand. They think organic is for rich people and hippies. And it's like, no, organic is what everything used to be before the petroleum industry infiltrated every industry we have.

I look at the detergent we're washing our clothes with. That's right. It's petroleum based. It's bad for a water supply with dust on top. Also petroleum based. And it's so yeah, once you become aware, you start looking around and seeing it everywhere and going, "Wow, this is not right. How are we going to How do we educate? How do we advocate? How do we bring awareness? How do we get every mom in America to wake up and say, 'Not my kid, not my house, not me.'"

**Laura:** Right. Yeah. Every woman in America, whether you have children or not, to say, "Absolutely not." And it's interesting to observe when new clients come in the different energy of I have to throw I just learned all this. I have to throw everything away and I have to start over and it's bug eyes and it's fear. I don't blame them. However, I go into this mode of what we're going to do is we're going to find non-toxic alternatives for everything you're currently using and then we're going to approach it step by step by step. You have a half bottle of that shampoo left. Finish it up and when you're done, then we'll get to this. Just slowing it down making it so it's longterm. It just isn't as effective if we jump into it like that and the money spent, I have to spend all this money and change everything and it's like we just kind of slowly ease them into it and maybe just pick one area of your life at a time.

**Aimee:** I think I started with shampoo. I started with shampoo when I was pregnant with Julian and I was like, I'm not going to use sodium lauryl sulfate anymore. And it was a great choice because my hair is healthier now than I ever was before. And I was 30 years old at the time, and here I am in my 40s and my hair is even healthier. One make one little change. Maybe change your detergent. Pick one little thing. Make one little change at a time. I like to think as you run out of what you're currently using, then let's replace that and we'll just go piece by piece.

It's back to what you were saying about organic food. I just saw this thing on social media and some guy goes, "How about if we all say instead of saying organic food, what if we say chemical food instead?" Like, what if we remove the it's just like vegetables or chemical vegetables? And we can really make a shift with our verbiage of do I choose the cucumber or the chemical cucumber? Really clicking that because right now there's so much stigma around, oh, organic makeup that's probably so much more expensive, but it's not. This is all propaganda and little brainwashing stuff that we can totally adjust with the words we put around it.

**Laura:** I love that. Yes. The neural linguistics. That's right. I love it. We change the words. We do the same thing with birth with the hypno babies birthing was you change the words and then you take the fear out of the thing and let's change the words with makeup. Is it makeup or is it chemical makeup? Am I going to wear eyeshadow or am I going to wear chemical eyeshadow?

**Aimee:** I love that. Changing the way we talk about things gives it way more power. Exactly. I love that Laura you're so profound, dude. It's exhausting.

**Laura:** Oh my gosh. Well, you've been fighting a battle for a really long time. This has been a long time. I feel like we're finally making some progress in this country with addressing these chemicals that have been in American products for such a long time and we're finally able to have conversations about this and say, "Ah, gross."

And what a beautiful outcome of the COVID debacle was the grand awakening of what chemicals are happening around me to see that we went through that really hard time of the shutdown and the great unknown and all the things and then when it's time to start opening back up now we're flooded with all new inquiry and it was like let's go.

**Aimee:** I think people started asking questions. I mean, I know when I made Momalicious, one of the things that shocked me the most was the fact that our FDA has no process for approving prenatal or any product in the US. So when I went to get approved in Canada, I had to go through Health Canada and I had to go through a very strict regulatory process where they looked at every single ingredient, looked at where that ingredient was coming from and had to approve every single one. And I think that we in America assume that that's what the FDA has been doing. We think that that's what our tax dollars have been paying for. And I can assure you that is not the case.

In America, we were using GRAS generally regarded as safe as a platform basically to approve everything. And I'm really glad that this current HHS secretary is taking all of the ingredients that are currently in the GRAS schedule and putting them under a microscope because red 40 is in there.

**Laura:** Oh god, thank God.

**Aimee:** There are toxic chemicals that are in this lineup that have never been looked at for safety. And it's about time. It's about time now that we've gotten to a point where it's what sperm count is estimated to be at zero in 10 years type of vibe.

**Laura:** Oh my gosh.

**Aimee:** And autism is 1 in 10. We've been squeezed to there's no choice. We have nothing left. Our bodies can only handle so much toxicity. At some point, our bodies are going to hit a level of toxicity with the things we're putting on our skin, the food that we're eating, the water we're drinking, the air we're breathing. At some point, the body just can't take it anymore.

Like you and I were talking about, the only time that anyone can get rid of dioxin, which comes from plastics exposure, is when a mother creates a placenta. The dioxin from her body goes into that placenta. And no wonder we're seeing autism rates the way that we're seeing them because the dioxin exposure to our firstborn children is exceptional. And you compound that with the additional additives, the petroleum based diet that we're ingesting, the wrappers on the foods, the cream we're putting on our skin, the perfume we're spraying on ourselves to smell pretty, the makeup we're putting on ourselves to make ourselves look pretty.

In a lot of ways, I think of the geishas in Japan during the Edo period. They wanted to be beautiful, so they were covering themselves in white powder. It was lead. Those women lived to be about 26 years old. They died of lead poisoning. At what point do we open our eyes and say enough is enough. Beauty is beautiful, but we can't allow beauty to kill us. Not at that cost.

**Laura:** Not at that cost. Yeah. Not at that cost. Yeah.
